data.title: PSK: Ultra-Long Duration Debt Component At Risk From Debt Cost Revaluations
data.description:
The State Street SPDR ICE Preferred Securities ETF (PSK) is a preferred securities ETF with primarily financial sector exposures at 67% of the portfolio.

Comparing to the iShares US Financials ETF (IYF), which represents a set of the equity stratum of financial exposures as opposed to just preferreds, preferreds and PSK have been more resilient in the face of this year's declines. We are a bit skeptical of the further resilience of the preferreds given some of the trends we are seeing in the yield curve. While there is an equity component to preferreds, there is the unignorable debt component as well—a very high duration debt component. In the face of re-inflation risks, duration bets are a bit of a concern. The equity component is also a concern with higher debt costs incoming and general economic pressure, which affects financial exposures. Therefore, we have concerns that the resilience of the preferreds might not hold.

PSK Breakdown

Preferred instruments tend to have a little higher expense ratio at 0.45% due to the more limited scope of the asset class. PSK is at 0.45%, and this is where comparable preferred security ETFs lie as well, with the (PFF) also at 0.45%. Financial institutions, which are more motivated to financial optimization since it determines risk-weighted assets, the amounts of reserves they have and need, and also the efficiency of their funding source costs with respect to their lending yields, make use of preferred securities more therefore those sectors dominate the exposures.

Necessarily, preferreds have an equity exposure and a debt exposure.

Bear Case

The bear case for the debt exposure is that duration is unsafe in the current environment where markets are re-evaluating their cost of capital expectations. We still don't have consumer inflation expectations logged yet, and the yield curve is already seeing significant upward shifts. The logic of the upward shifts is that both long- and short-term rate expectations are changing. In the short term it's clearly the oil price upside to inflation, the rule of thumb being $10 in oil price per barrel contributes 0.4% to CPI. The war may escalate further with more US troops being posted to the region and with strikes continuing with no obvious abatement. It's unclear whether meaningful ceasefire talks are taking place. The short term also has implications for the longer-term rates, in addition to general conflict and geopolitical fracture premia likely being placed on longer-term structural rates as well due to the inflationary impact of such things. With all rates across the yield curve going up, very long-duration bets, among them preferreds, all have price downside through the debt component.

For the equity component, demand destruction from higher oil prices and the higher debt costs as determined by the market, along with upward pressure on credit spreads, all reflect pressure that could impact the bulk of the exposures in PSK, namely financials. These demand-side risks also spill over into debt and duration risks through credit spreads, which put upward pressure on YTMs and endanger duration. While higher than they have been lately, they are still not at Ukraine war levels, which may be an eventual level that the current conflict could bring, particularly with the consumer weaker than it was in 2022. So maybe there is credit spread upside at the BBB rating level around which the PSK's exposures are concentrated in terms of credit rating. Financials are in general exposed on the equity side to any macro concerns therefore we see downside in the equity component.

Bull Case

The bull case is that maybe ships will start passing through the Strait unharassed by Iran. This may be due to diplomatic successes or Iran's own geopolitical designs. If the hostilities end, that would also achieve the same end. While it may take some time for Gulf capacity to be recovered, there is also hope of more oil production coming from other OPEC+ members who were in the process of unwinding supply cuts and would have excess capacity. This would contribute favorably both to the cost of capital outlook to help the debt component of preferreds and the PSK as well as the equity component through eliminating the demand-side risk of unavoidably higher prices, for example, at the pump, for US consumers.

But we don't see this as particularly likely at the moment and are concerned about troop buildup, among other signs that the conflict cannot be easily resolved at this point.

Bottom Line

The key thing is that the yield curve is shifting upwards across the board, including in the areas that would affect ultra-long duration bets like preferreds, which have no fixed maturity. In addition to that, there is no concurrent help being provided to the equity exposures either. Therefore, the relative resilience of the PSK, particularly with duration meaning sensitivity to long-term rate assumptions by markets, may not be sustained from this point. We'd be more comfortable with instruments that have at least fallen already and negatively responded to the energy crisis. On that basis we'd simply not hazard money here, particularly when cash appears to be favorable in an environment where things could get worse before they get better.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Boosted By Powerful Pricing And Volume Tailwinds
data.description:
Elevator Pitch

I think Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) is seeing some powerful growth catalysts:

  1. Upstream O&G is the major driver of Exxon Mobil's earnings.
  2. Escalating Iran war tensions provide a favorable backdrop for oil prices.
  3. Production volumes can grow rapidly from low-cost upstream assets.
  4. XOM stock is valued richly vs. peers.
  5. Don't fight the clear and powerful uptrend.

Upstream O&G Is the Major Driver of Exxon Mobil's Earnings

Exxon Mobil operates in 4 key segments:

  1. Upstream: Exploration, production, and sale of oil, natural gas, and LNG.
  2. Energy Products: Refining crude oil derivatives and selling fuels such as gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel.
  3. Chemical Products: Using hydrocarbons to make and sell petrochemicals such as plastics.
  4. Specialty Products: Similar to chemical products but making more specialized materials such as industrial lubricants and greases.

My thesis is focused on the upstream segment. I want to highlight that this is the most important piece for Exxon Mobil's earnings, as it makes up on average around 65% of GAAP segmental earnings:

Escalating Iran War Tensions Provide a Favorable Backdrop for Oil Prices

The Iran war is escalating as the U.S. is getting closer to deploying troops on the ground to potentially safeguard the Strait of Hormuz (a key blockaded chokepoint through which 20% of global oil supply passes), seize Kharg Island (a key Iranian military site and export terminal), or conduct operations against Iran's uranium site in Isfahan to reduce the country's nuclear threat. Already, 3,500 U.S. troops have arrived in the Middle East, and Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf's rhetoric is... aggressive, to put it mildly.

For the global oil markets, the Strait of Hormuz blockade is the most critical element. Here's some perspective on the scale of the supply shortage this is creating:

A simple back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests the closure of the strait is reducing global oil flows by some 11 million barrels a day, after accounting for the interventions so far aimed at offsetting the loss. When compared with pre-war demand levels, that leaves a roughly 9 million-barrel shortfall — a yawning gap that is more than the oil consumption of the UK, France, Germany, Spain and Italy combined.

- Bloomberg News (emphasis added).

Another clear sign of escalation and potential further supply constraints to the global oil market is the fact that Iran-backed Houthi groups in Yemen are joining the conflict, firing missiles at Israel and threatening to choke off the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, which is another key global shipping route that sees 10-12% of global maritime trade pass through it:

As a result of these escalations, I expect both WTI Crude Oil (CL1:COM) and Brent Oil (CO1:COM) to continue increasing and remain above $100/bbl at a minimum:

According to Bloomberg, the U.S. government and Wall Street are starting to more seriously consider the prospect of $200/bbl oil prices. Obviously, this is a major windfall tailwind for upstream O&G players like Exxon Mobil. My analysis shows that its oil commodity realization prices have virtually perfect correlation to Crude and Brent prices:

Production Volumes Can Grow Rapidly From Low-Cost Upstream Assets

Exxon Mobil is increasing upstream production at a rapid clip of almost 10% YoY on a 4-quarter average basis:

In recent quarters, the company tended to rather consistently deliver modest production surprises vs. consensus for total average daily production in millions of barrels of oil equivalents (MBOE):

The company's upstream production sites in the Permian Basin and Guyana made up over half of the company's total upstream production in 2025. And these 2 sites are expected to increase production volumes at a rapid clip over the next 5 years. For example, Exxon's production at the Permian Basin is expected to grow at 9.3% CAGR:

For Guyana, the company is on track to increase production capacity to 1.7M BOE/d by 2030. Given this, I'm assuming a production cadence of 1.6, as I believe production utilization will be high, but it's a bit aggressive to assume 100%. This still leads to a very rapid 18.0% CAGR growth estimate over the next 5 years:

The best part is that these 2 sites have low production costs. For example, the breakeven oil price level for the Guyana site is already at $30/bbl. And the company is targeting a global breakeven oil cost of $30/bbl by 2030, enabled a lot by more than 40 technological innovations to improve operational efficiency in the Permian Basin operations.

For context on how low these breakeven costs are, here's what the surveyed breakeven oil cost level was among 92 US upstream O&G firms and locations:

The bottom line is that there are plenty of volume tailwinds ahead as well.

XOM Stock is Valued Richly vs. Peers

XOM's 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA is at 10.0x, which corresponds to a 49% premium vs. its comps' median multiple of 6.7x:

This chart below shows that XOM is trading at a much higher premium vs. its comps than the typical 27%, which is an argument for relatively stretched valuations:

Don't Fight the Clear and Powerful Uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

XOM Technical Analysis

This is very simple. The XOM monthly chart shows a very clear, powerful uptrend. And there is no reason to fight the trend, as there are zero signs of sellers or bearish pressure.

Takeaway and Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to compelling pricing and volume catalysts.
  • Valuation: Bearish due to rich valuation vs. peers.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish due to impressive bullish momentum with no signs of sellers.
  • Final XOM Assessment and Rating: Bullish, "Buy."

data.title: 2 Dividend Growers Providing Inflation-Beating Income Boost
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

As income-focused investors, we tend to get categorized more heavily as those who seek high yields. However, that isn't always the case. There are those of us, like myself, who view ourselves as income-focused investors but also want dividend growth. To achieve that, my portfolio tends to be split between those sorts of positions that provide relatively higher yields and those that focus more specifically on growth prospects.

Dividend growth provides what I believe are two key advantages for investors. First, it can help to combat the negative headwinds of inflation over the long term, which reduces the overall buying power. Second, I believe it also helps to combat the dividend cutters that one may have in their portfolio. When incorporating higher-yielding instruments, it generally is a matter of "when" and not "if" you'll see a cut.

Balancing dividend growers into an income portfolio helps to see cash flow moving higher over the long term. Though one can also reinvest, which can further help to boost the overall cash flow growth passively as well.

Today, we are going to touch on 2 of those names that have been able to provide significant dividend growth. Not only that, but these are names that I also suspect are able to continue to deliver that sort of meaningful growth as well. Supported by management's own target and expectations moving forward on the back of earnings growth.

#1 Microsoft (MSFT): 10%+ Dividend Growth, and That Can Continue

To start off, we can start with the most well-known, MSFT. The share price has been under incredible pressure as the technology sector as a whole takes a breather on the back of some AI skepticism. However, that has only made this a more interesting name to consider now, as a lot of the froth in the valuation has been removed, in my opinion. Shares are now trading well under their longer-term average forward earnings multiple level.

On the dividend front, the company has delivered over 20 years of consecutive annual dividend growth now.

Even after the decline in share price, the yield is sitting at only around 0.92%. However, my main focus was on the actual growth that it has been able to deliver and not on the dividend yield alone. For the last 3, 5, and 10 years, MSFT has delivered a 10%+ CAGR.

This does look set to easily continue, given the expectation for earnings growth continues to be over 10% for the next several years, according to analyst estimates.

Free cash flow has been under more pressure as spending to fund AI has ramped up considerably. Though with that increase in spending, it is exactly what is expected to fuel the earnings growth for MSFT in the first place. Further, FCF is expected to be flat this year before also rising sharply moving forward.

Thus, another good sign that exemplary dividend growth can continue. This is dividend growth that is well above the stated inflation headlines we see as well, which can be very important.

#2 NextEra Energy (NEE): 10%+ Dividend Growth, But Slow Down Targeted

NEE is another fairly well-known name for income investors. This is an energy infrastructure company with a focus on generating power from renewable sources. They operate through their Florida Power & Light Company, as well as the NextEra Energy Resources LLC. While MSFT is busy investing in AI, building out data centers, and other infrastructure projects, it is requiring increased power. The power that NEE can generate is thus an infrastructure company set to benefit as well.

That said, unlike MSFT, NEE's share price has been heading in a different direction, which has seen its valuation become less appealing relative to where it had been trading. At the same time, it was after a collapse in the premium valuation that they had been receiving from around the end of 2019 to early 2023. The renewable energy trade hype and higher interest rate environment made NEE less appealing at that time to investors. As utilities become more appealing once again, NEE has seen its share price slowly grinding upward once again.

Of course, long-term investors don't really care about trading the name anyway. The day-to-day, or even year-to-year, share price isn't too much of a concern. Instead, we can have the vision to hold through thick and thin. Making it easier to hold through those thin times is collecting that dividend along the way while the share price sorts itself out based on growth in the future.

Based on the current share price, the yield comes in at 2.7%—which may not be all that impressive to income investors that solely seek high yields, but it is still quite appealing. The S&P 500 Index itself provides only around a 1.1% dividend yield. Though the utility sector often provides higher yields of 3%+ over the sector median. Still, NEE is a much more growth-oriented utility name.

We are now at over 30 years of consecutive dividend increases for this company, which has provided a decade of ~11.3% CAGR for the dividend. More recently, the growth has been 10%, but that is exactly the target that management was going for. However, growth is expected to moderate for the next couple of years. Management is now going for a target of 6%, which is still a healthy level.

The management team believes that they can grow their earnings 8%+ over the longer term, and that is around the level that they have been able to deliver on historically, too. Analysts also provide a similar outlook based on their estimates.

With all that said, we could be in a position one day to see NEE be able to deliver 10% dividend growth once again or use that 6% growth target as a base level—which is still likely to have a good chance to outpace inflation moving forward.

data.title: The S&P 500 Fell Almost 9%, And I Took The Opportunity To Buy More (Here's Why)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American assets. This article is part of a weekly series where I bring valuable insights about economics and investments to SA readers. The Iran War is already over a month old, and its impact on the stock market is already greater than expected. In these articles we will understand the opportunities and risks of this new scenario.

Context

The S&P 500 reached 6,368 points at the end of last week, the lowest point of the year. For the year, the index fell 6.96%, and since the peak reached on January 28, the index has fallen 8.73%, and as oil closes another week above $100, investors are beginning to become extremely worried.

Some analysts have even published an article indicating that we have entered a bear market, and my objective with this article is to separate what is a correction from what is a bear market, as well as to give my perceptions about the market moment and address the new risks of the investment thesis.

Corrections and Bear Markets

Firstly, it is important to differentiate a correction from a bear market. As the data below suggests, the market has experienced 105 pullbacks since World War II. Of these 105 events, only 13 became bear markets (drop above 20%), and 26 were just corrections (drop up to 10%).

This means on 66 occasions they did not go beyond the pullback. We are at that level now. Will the market fall between 5 and 10% and recover? The statistical chances are much more likely for a recovery, which would imply a resolution or reduction in tensions in the Middle East.

Volatility Matters!

It is interesting to note that other events that generated a rise in yields and a fall in shares quickly resulted in actions by the Trump administration to reduce sentiment. If we take the past as a basis, the combination of events (Iran War) with a fall in the stock market (impact on family assets) could lead to a resolution or de-escalation of the conflict in April.

The Opportunities

Many investors follow my weekly series and know that I defend an optimistic view for the American stock market. My target for the S&P 500 is 7778 points, and I remain convinced of my investment thesis.

Many investors who are skeptical today were skeptical in the past when stocks were rising, especially in the technology sector. At the time, skeptical investors complained about the stretched valuation and the strong concentration in a few companies.

Well, the latest events have made the technology sector trade at the same multiples as the S&P 500. Do you prefer to invest at discounted multiples or at highs?

This concludes the optimistic arguments for this thesis, and of course, I will be paying attention to inflation and activity indicators to highlight the real impacts of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz on the economy. Now, let's analyze the risks; after all, new risks are emerging.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

It is not possible to clearly state who is winning the war in Iran. Although American forces have shown power of action and dealt a severe blow to the Iranian regime, Iran apparently gained more bargaining power than was believed before the conflict.

Iran was not expected to discredit its forces amid this attack, but the main point regarding investments is that Iran has shown a real ability to close the Strait of Hormuz, and this has a major impact on the market's risk premium given the geographic importance of the location for the global oil market.

The fact is that the Strait was closed, and European or Gulf countries did not move to reopen it. This suggests that Iran possesses a powerful weapon and great bargaining power that was previously unrealized.

The Bottom Line

I imagine few investors believed the conflict in Iran would drag on for so long. Well, we have been talking about this for more than 30 days, and although there is a ceasefire for negotiations, there is still no clear sign that the war will end.

The fact is that when the right scenario occurs, the market will have already priced it in, and historical experience shows that there is little likelihood of an even bigger drawdown at that point. Not to mention that the valuation of American companies has become much more attractive at this moment.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. The fall in prices made valuations more attractive, and consequently, the risk-return ratio on investment improved considerably.

data.title: Another Monday Madness: A Tech Take
data.description:
Monday Madness

Another Monday comes with another post on Truth Social from President Trump. We’re covering the macro picture every Monday as we push through the current U.S.-Israel-Iran war. This Monday, not unlike last Monday, the U.S. remains in “serious discussions” with Iran. Trump's five-day pause on hitting energy sites amid productive conversations with Iran, announced last Monday, got extended until April 6th on Thursday as we neared the deadline. These Monday headlines also make it clear that this administration’s vulnerability lies in financial markets, particularly as we see them begin to digest the gravity of the situation.

Unfortunately for Trump, the market isn’t buying into his rhetoric anymore, and a big part of why is that Iran has been denying its occurrence. Markets went up slightly after his post on Thursday post-market close, but gains didn’t hold, and another crash came around Friday, driving Nasdaq and Dow into correction territory, and it has seriously spilled into Monday. Oil prices (CO1:COM) are hitting their highest level since 2022, and panic seems to be growing roots. Oil prices are driving the physical shortage that has yet to fully trickle down to the consumer, although prices at the pump are not hiding how bad things could get, as shown below.

We’re also hearing divergent accounts of the state of the Strait, with Trump claiming that Iran has allowed up to 20 tankers to pass through the Strait as a “present,” while other reports indicate that two vessels from China Ocean Shipping Company were unable to pass through. Footage circulating online showcases just how bad the traffic buildup at the Strait has become. There are now almost 17.8M barrels per day that usually pass through Hormuz, and that flow is disrupted.

With the war now entering its 31st day, the question on everyone's mind is: Are we near the end? More specifically, are we near that April buying moment we got after Liberation Day?

All signs point to Trump not wanting a ground invasion or a more serious escalation, but unlike prior situations we’ve seen Trump navigate, his counterpart is uninterested, which leads us to believe things could stretch out longer. The Pentagon apparently is preparing for a limited ground operation, particularly one involving Kharg Island and coastal sites near Hormuz, indicating this is the arrival of the USS Tripoli in the region, bringing about 3,500 more U.S. troops. A ground intervention, even in a limited capacity, would make matters significantly worse on multiple fronts.

For starters, Iran is threatening that any such invasion would be met with force and the creation of a new potential chokepoint at the Bab al-Mandeb Strait. The closure of the latter would materially worsen the current oil disruption. About 4.2M barrels of crude oil and petroleum products pass through the Bab al-Mandeb Strait daily, according to H1 '25 stats. This number is already down from its prior height after the Houthis' blockade of the Strait in 2023. Yet, with the ongoing conflict and closure of Hormuz, a lot of traffic has been going through Bab al-Mandeb, Saudi Arabia’s Red Sea port of Yanbu, and exports alone have tripled to 4M per day. So, Bab al-Mandeb is another leverage card that has yet to be played and could knock this oil disruption into a catastrophe, as the combined closure of the two Straits would block about 30% of global container shipping and 22% of global oil supply.

Secondly, this would have disastrous connotations for the Trump administration's standing at home. Trump's approval has hit 36%, the lowest point seen since he returned to office for his second term. The driver for this sharp drop in approval, as shown below, is the entry into and conduct of this war. A ground invasion would only weigh this approval rate lower. The rate of those who approve of the war is also coming down from 37% last week to 35% now, while the disapproval rate has gone in the opposite direction, growing from 59% to 61%.

The Trump element is not divorced from how markets are digesting this war, nor is the sentiment in financial markets isolated from how the president is conducting it. The more the impact of oil disruptions and potential ground invasion is felt by the end consumer and thus reflected on markets, the more urgency the Trump administration will feel to make an exit. So, as the backlash of this war grows higher, we think the end grows nearer, too.

Tech Sluggishness Pre-Dates The War

For us, everything comes back to technology, and this war is not without impact on the tech sector. We discussed this in our last piece, "Another Uno-Reverse Card: This Market Trusts Trump," with specific focus on the impact of helium shortage, with about 30% of the global supply coming from Qatar. Chip makers such as TSMC (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF), and SK Hynix all have material helium dependency, with the Korean fabs holding 65% dependency on Qatar’s Helium supply. TSMC’s LNG imports from Qatar add more pressure, with a 33.7% reliance. Matters have trickled into other areas, too, including Aluminum. Iran targeted the two main aluminum producers over the past 24 hours: the UAE and Bahrain. Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) and Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) both confirmed that their facilities have been hit, with the latter going so far as to cite “significant damage.” The attacks pushed up aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) close to its four-year peak, as the Gulf accounts for about 9% of the global aluminum supply. According to analysts at ING Economics:

The latest attacks increase the probability of a prolonged disruption scenario, where supply losses could persist even if geopolitical tensions ease, reinforcing upside risks to ​prices.

This matters for a couple of different reasons, specifically when it comes to tech, where aluminum is everywhere in server chassis, EV batteries, packaging, and even within data center infrastructure. Packaging alone accounts for +21% of aluminum shipments in North America. So there is a very real supply constraint that is playing out of this, and that will take weeks if not months to resolve, even after the war is over.

The other concern is that outside of AI, tech end demand remains pretty sluggish. IDC forecasted smartphone TAM growth of 1.2% for the year last September. That outlook was revised down to a -0.9% decline in December, citing memory shortages and the pushout of Apple’s new iPhone lineup. Since then, IDC cut numbers again, and this time more dramatically, for a -12.9% decline, and it was described by IDC as "not a temporary squeeze, but a tsunami-like shock originating in the memory supply chain.” The outlook for a -12.9% decline in smartphone TAM is the one that stuck and represents the lowest annual smartphone shipment volume in over a decade.

Things aren’t much better on the PC front, either. IDC’s baseline outlook, shared last November, was for a -2.4% outlook. This was revised down to -11.3% in March, with industry peers pointing to as much as a -15% outlook, citing “massive disruption.” The AI build-out is driving memory costs substantially higher and weighing on end demand. So, this adds another layer to the current tech market: higher memory ASP is bad for device TAM, and as the end consumer digests this, pricing power for non-AI memory wanes as units fall. According to Wccftech, DDR5 retail prices are already falling. If memory spot prices have hit a ceiling, then this also means bad news for memory players, who have been the growth spot for tech since the start of the year.

Where To Hide Out In Tech?

The analog peer group doesn’t get too much love; it bridges the gap between the physical and digital, feeding into several diversified end markets (including auto, industrial, consumer, IoT, defense, and healthcare). For the most part, with analog chips, we’re talking about older process nodes (i.e., they don’t often fall within the 3nm-and-smaller conversation) because of the physical properties required to perform their functions. So, this makes them a less attractive area to explore when the whole market is moving on the AI infrastructure build-out, with the exception of guys like Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) or even Infineon, the former of which very early on in this AI boom carved out a play within the AI supply chain for power management (the content of which has been increasing). Yet, in times like these, when we’re seeing increased macro uncertainty and fears of higher component costs as the oil disruption tied to Iran persists, analogs become something of a safe haven.

The thesis behind this is pretty simple, and it’s been tested and tried. When there are panics over price pressure, shortages, and longer lead times, everyone and their grandmother will pull in orders, or even double orders, to secure capacity at a more favorable price. Now, this happens across the supply chain, but the reason analog is special is that, unlike other sectors, analog tech doesn’t easily go out of fashion. Remember, we’re not talking about 5nm moving to 3nm; we’re talking about old generations. So, the backlash for holding more inventory than ideal on these chips if end demand doesn’t kick in is more manageable.

Hence, when macro uncertainty looms, analogs tend to see short-term benefit from pull-ins and double ordering. We saw this happen around this time last year in the aftermath of Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs. Remember, Trump announced a package of import duties, including a universal 10% tariff and higher tariffs for about 60 countries in the double-digit range. The market melted in reaction before the 90-day pause, which took place a few days later. Nonetheless, when the next quarter’s print came around, analog companies across the board boasted better-than-expected numbers, which soon appeared to be the result of pull-ins and double ordering out of fear that tariffs were coming once the pause was over.

For example, last year Texas Instruments (TXN) reported Q1 '25 results in late April, beating expectations and guiding above seasonal for Q2, pushing the stock up 10% in reaction. The following quarter, Q2, the company guided below seasonality for Q3, which pointed to the double ordering that propped up results the quarter prior.

While we’re not in this exact situation today, given that last April’s episode was more of a demand and supply shock, and today we’re seeing more of a supply shock, we share similarities with it. The most important of which is that markets are stuck in another round of macroeconomic uncertainty that could seriously impact supply chains due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

In this environment, we think we’ll see pull-ins and double orders turn their heads again, and with that, we see opportunity in the analog peer group and greater short-term resilience. Given that each analog name has its own unique exposure to end markets, we think the best way to play this thesis is to stick to the guys with either 1. The most diversified end market exposure that’ll benefit from across-the-board pull-in without being overly exposed to one end market demand. Here, we like TXN, which has exposure to Industrial (33% of total sales), auto (33% of total sales), data center (9% of total sales), personal electronics (21% of total sales), and embedded and communications infrastructure (together 4% of total sales). 2. More concentrated end market exposure in favorable areas, such as Analog Devices (ADI) or Microchip (MCHP). The industrial and auto markets are most prone to panic ordering and hoarding because if you’re missing even one component, the assembly line pauses, as analog is often sole-sourced, meaning pieces are designed to fit a specific structure.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NIHI: Extracting Dividends From International Equities Via A Covered Call ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The NEOS MSCI EAFE High Income ETF

The past few years have seen the popularization of options-based strategies, and Neos Investments has been at the center of an interesting trend. Neos is famous in the ETF space for using put spreads to enhance returns for a plethora of asset classes, but they also have a number of robust classic buy-write funds (covered call ETFs). The NEOS MSCI EAFE High Income ETF (NIHI) is such a fund. The ETF is a very useful tool to extract dividends from a portfolio of international equities. In today's article we are going to go through the larger topic of covered call ETFs, the NIHI fund build and structure, and its application in various macro cycles.

What Are Covered Call ETFs?

Let us start with some basic concepts around covered call ETFs in general:

A covered call ETF is an exchange-traded fund that generates potential income by writing call options on the securities the ETF holds. These actively-managed ETFs give investors the opportunity to benefit from covered call writing without having to participate in the options market directly.

Covered call ETFs allow investors to earn income in the form of options contract premiums, in addition to any other dividends, and potentially reduce portfolio volatility. One trade-off is that upside potential may be limited if call options are exercised — typically when the underlying security reaches the strike price — which could result in shares being called away from the fund.

So the main purpose of a well-run covered call ETF is to extract dividends from a pool of assets or ETFs when the underlying holdings might not have any. The idea is to use options in order to generate said dividends. Writing calls simply allows the fund to use the options market to do that.

Despite many misconceptions, covered call ETFs tend to have similar long-term total returns as the underlying assets, and this is for well-run funds. Poorly run covered call structures trail their holdings from a total return perspective. And now here come the nuances in terms of how a covered call fund is built.

Types Of Covered Call ETFs

Systematic

Systematic covered call ETFs have rules-based ways of overlaying their options. The best example is the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD). We covered this ETF at length here. This fund is structured to follow the Cboe S&P 500 BuyWrite Index:

The Cboe S&P 500 BuyWrite Index (BXM) is designed to measure the total rate of return of a hypothetical “covered call” strategy applied to the S&P 500 Index. This strategy consists of a hypothetical portfolio consisting of a “long” position indexed to the S&P 500 Index on which are deemed sold a succession of one month, at-the-money call options on the S&P 500 Index listed on the Cboe Options Exchange. We refer to this hypothetical portfolio as the “covered S&P 500 Index portfolio.”

So the buy-write index and inherently the ETF write one-month rolling at-the-money call options. This is a systematic strategy because it does not care if the market is oversold, overbought, or has a high implied volatility priced in the options chain. Day in and day out, this strategy will do the same transactions.

In our article we show investors how systematic strategies as used by XYLD trail active ones:

This tells us that active funds are better than systematic ones in this instance, and constantly rolling 1-month calls is not the best way to extract dividends from the S&P 500. Therefore, in our opinion XYLD will be a mediocre long-term performer, especially due to the fund's inability to monetize short-term trends or events, items which active funds can correctly manage.

Which brings us to active approaches in terms of covered calls.

Active

Active covered call ETFs can choose their strikes, maturity dates, and coverage ratios when overlaying options. While nobody can time the market, extreme situations are very obvious for all asset managers. If a market is oversold when looking at a multitude of technical indicators, managers might choose to sell calls on only a portion of the portfolio, riding a bounce up in price via NAV and then writing calls on the remaining portion.

Similarly, bear markets are sometimes characterized by high implied volatility, which in turn means higher option premiums. This structuring aspect can be used to maximize profits on the strategy during such occurrences.

Thirdly, managers can speculate overbought conditions by writing options with in-the-money strikes or longer maturities in order to increase the deltas of the written calls, thus taking advantage of any correction.

The main takeaway for readers is that active covered call ETFs are better long-term if the management teams in place are solid.

NIHI Build And Structure

The ETF has a very straightforward structure:

The fund contains a large position in the iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF (IEFA), which is overlaid with two separate call options. IEFA is an international equities ETF that aims to do the following:

The iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid- and small-capitalization developed market equities, excluding the U.S. and Canada.

The index referenced by IEFA is the MSCI EAFE IMI Index. Do note that NIHI does not write calls on the IEFA ETF directly but writes them on the underlying index:

The Fund seeks to take advantage of tax loss harvesting opportunities in addition to utilizing Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower 60/40 tax rates.

In the holdings table above, we can see the ETF writing calls on the index at two separate strikes, but both with an April 17, 2026 maturity date.

The main holding in the fund, IEFA, is a well-diversified international equities ETF, with the following geographic exposure:

The ETF represents a staple of international equities (excluding U.S. and Canada exposure), and long-term is well-correlated with the competing Vanguard product, Vanguard Developed Markets Index Fund (VEA):

These ETFs are used by institutional and retail investors as building blocks for obtaining international equities exposure. All that NIHI does is use the covered call structure to extract dividends from the underlying equities.

Some fund analytics for NIHI:

  • AUM: $155M.
  • Expense ratio: 0.68%.
  • Distribution rate: 10%.
  • Distribution period: monthly.
  • Fund CUSIP: 78433H543.

Tracking The Fund's Performance

The fund is fairly new, having come to market in late September 2025. Since inception, the name has tracked the underlying ETF closely:

We can see from the above chart that NIHI has a total return profile very well correlated with the iShares Core MSCI EAFE ETF (IEFA), which it tracks. The period of data is still very small, but as expected, we see correlations breaking down during significant spikes in performance for IEFA, like in February 2026. Given its call writing, NIHI has a capped upside during short-term spikes in value for the underlying ETF.

However, as we can see from the chart above, longer-term the two instruments exhibit very similar total returns, which is the defining characteristic of well-run active covered call ETFs.

Covered Call ETFs During Bear Markets

The one structural aspect to keep in mind with respect to covered call ETFs is the propensity for the price and NAV to move lower than the underlying holdings during bear markets. The simple reason behind this occurrence is the fact that the covered call ETF tends to transform equity returns into dividends, and when there are no equity returns to be had, the fund just disburses return of capital, which chips away at the NAV.

We can clearly see that tendency in the above graph with the price returns for IEFA and NIHI in 2026. IEFA is negative for the year, but NIHI's price return is much lower. While on a total return basis they are quite close, on a price basis a covered call ETF will always significantly underperform during a bear market. Why? Because it disburses returns that are not there.

Over long cycles, good covered call ETFs will have similar total returns as the underlying, do expect lower NAV performance during bear markets, which means higher negative profit and loss figures in your brokerage account versus just holding an underlying like IEFA outright.

Reference Literature.

Please find below a number of research articles on the topic of covered call ETFs:

  • Pros and Cons of a Covered Call ETF — and When to Buy.
  • Covered Call Primer.
  • Covered Call ETFs: A Beginner's Guide.

Key Advantages And Disadvantages

Advantages:

  • Getting dividends from a portfolio of international equities.
  • Active option writing and structuring.
  • Monthly distributions.

Disadvantages:

  • Potential tracking error versus owning IEFA outright.
  • NAV decreases during bear markets.
  • High expense ratio.

Conclusion

NIHI is a covered call ETF from Neos. The ETF represents an elegant solution to extracting dividends from international equities via the covered call structure. NIHI holds a position in IEFA, which is overlaid with index options to optimize the tax treatment of dividends. The fund has tracked IEFA fairly well on a total return basis so far, with the name being an active covered call ETF. Neos has a very good track record in the space, so we expect NIHI to exhibit a total return very closely correlated with IEFA long term. The name is best suited for investors looking for monthly income from the international equities asset class.

This article answers three main questions about NIHI:

  • Is NIHI actively managed, or does it track an index?
  • What type of investor is NIHI most suitable for?
  • How is NIHI likely to perform in different market conditions?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Wells Fargo: 5% Capital Appreciation Potential From WFC.PR.L Preferred Shares
data.description:
Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) is a financial company that provides financial, investment, banking, and mortgage products. Its current market capitalization is above $243.8 billion. At the end of 2025, the company had close to $2.148 trillion in total assets and close to $443.5 billion in total debt. The preferred equity is around $16.6 billion, and the total equity is ~ $183 billion. WFC is rated as "investment-grade" by the major credit agencies. Below you can see their credit rating:

WFC has 26 consecutive years of uninterrupted dividend payments, as the last 4 of them are consecutive dividend increases. The total dividend paid to WFC's common shareholders over the past twelve months is $1.75. The current price of the stock is $79.02, which corresponds to a dividend yield of 2.21%. The common shares outstanding are close to 3.1 billion, so with a $1.80 forward dividend per share, the company has distributed approximately $5.6 billion in common shares over the last year. The preferred stocks' dividends are close to $1.05 billion, or around 19.3% of the common stock distributions.

And in the next price chart, you can see how the market evaluates WFC's common stock:

Actually, in this article, we will pay more attention to the one traditional convertible preferred stock, Wells Fargo & Company PERP PFD CNV A (WFC.PR.L) of the company.

WFC.PR.L

Firstly, some basic details for the stock.

WFC.PR.L is a non-cumulative perpetual convertible traditional stock that was issued on 04/14/2008 (3.5 million shares). It pays non-cumulative distributions of 7.5%, or a $75 annual amount (paid in quarterly amounts). Moody's rates the stock with a "Baa2" credit rating, S&P with a "BB+," and Fitch with "BBB." The stock was originally issued by Wachovia Corp. and traded under the ticker symbol WBP.PR.L. Under the terms of the prospectus, the issue is perpetual, with each share convertible into 32.0513 shares of common stock at a conversion price of $31.20. No redemption was contemplated. The only possibility for conversion was at the company's initiative—provided that the market price of the common stock exceeded 130% of the conversion price for 20 trading sessions within every 30 consecutive trading days.

After the 2008 stock crash of Wachovia Corp. and the subsequent takeover by Wells Fargo & Co., the preferred stock effectively became a "busted convertible"—that is, perpetual preferred stock with no real prospect of conversion. In addition to the lack of maturity, the stock also does not have a call option, which means that the achieved yield is fixed for an indefinite period of time - practically forever. The current yield of the stock is around 6.55%. In the next chart, you can see the potential prices and current yields of WFC.PR.L:

The Other Financial Instruments of WFC

We cannot fail to introduce you to the company's other financial instruments in a few words.

- OTC Bonds

In the next screenshot, the WFC's OTC bonds are shown with their yields to maturity and credit ratings:

The highest duration bond has a yield to maturity of close to 6.26% and an "A3" credit rating by Moody's and a "BBB" credit rating by S&P. Its spread to the treasury yield currently is close to 1.3%.

- Preferred Stocks

WFC has five other perpetual non-cumulative preferred stocks:

All are traded currently below par. Their current yields are between 5.97% and 6.35%. Moody's rates the stocks with a "Baa2" credit rating, S&P with a "BB+," and Fitch with a "BBB." All are callable, except (WFC.PR.D), whose call date is on 07/20/2026. The spread to the treasury yield of the stock is close to 1.42% if we take the higher current yield of the group.

Comparison With Peers

Below is shown a comparison between WFC.PR.L and Bank of America Corporation 7.25% CNV PFD L (BAC.PR.L) — we see that they have historically performed similarly. At the same time, rating agencies do not agree about the two stocks. S&P and Fitch give a lower rating to WFC.PR.L compared to BAC.PR.L ("BBB-" by S&P and "BBB+" by Fitch), while Moody's believes that the two stocks carry the same credit risk. In addition, Bank of America's preferred stock trades at a slightly lower current yield than Wells Fargo's (BAC.PR.L, at the time of writing, trades at around 6.08% current yield), which also speaks in favor of BAC. BAC.PR.L was in the same situation as WFC.PR.L for a long time — investors ignored it because of its high nominal value and preferred other BAC's preferred stocks.

However, over time, this changed, and BAC.PR.L was fairly priced compared to the other instruments of BAC, and we believe that the same would be happening with WFC.PR.L- to be fairly priced to the other financial instruments of its family.

Given the lack оf a call оptiоn, WFC.PR.L is expected tо trade at a lower yield compared to the other financial instruments in the family. The call оptiоn brings аdditional prоfitability to the investоr as cоmpensation fоr the assumed risk of еarly rеdеmptiоn - in its аbsence, this premium is eliminated, which pushes the yield dоwn compared tо cаllable analоgues. The lоwest nominal yield among WFC's preferred stоcks is 4.25% by WFC.PR.D, and it has a current yield of close to 6.25%. Since WFC.PR.D has the lоwest nominal yield; the cоmpаny hаs the leаst incеntive tо refinance this issuе, which mаkes its call option the one with the lоwest value. A cаll оption is a negative fоr invеstors because it givеs thе company the right tо buy bаck the stocks at unfаvorаble conditions — therefore, the lоwer thе vаlue оf this оptiоn, the better it is for investоrs. This is why stоcks with lоwer cаll оptiоns price trade at lower yields compared to stocks with higher call options, all else bеing equal.

Since WFC-L has no call option at all, it would be logical to assume it should be fairly priced with CY at least as low as WFC-D's. If WFC.PR.L were to trаde аt WFC.PR.D's current yield, its price wоuld be clоse to $1,200, implying approximately 5% capital appreciation potential from current levels.

Conclusion

WFC.PR.L is a pеrpetual cоnvertible prefеrred stоck with no call option and no real prospect of cоnvеrsion, making it a "forever" investment with a cоupоn of 7.5%. Its currеnt yiеld of clоse to 6.55% is higher than that of other WFC preferred stocks (5.97%–6.35%), which is unjustifiеd givеn the lаck of cаll risk — that is, investors should аccеpt a lower yiеld for it compared to callable cоunterparts, not a higher оne. If the market revalues ​​the stock to the levels of the other instrumеnts in the fаmily, the stоck would have 5% potential for capital appreciation frоm current levels. That's why we think WFC.PR.D is undervalued.

data.title: Origin Bancorp Is Getting Close To A Downgrade
data.description:
One bank that has really surprised me over the last several months now has been Origin Bancorp, Inc. (OBK). As a concentrated player that focuses on the valuable Sunbelt region of the United States, it has established for itself a history of modest growth. Most of its business involves the state of Texas, which itself is a highly desirable part of the nation because of low taxation and warm weather. But even with these characteristics, I ended up assigning it a "Hold" rating when I first wrote about it in June of last year. From that time through today, the stock has actually drastically outperformed the market, jumping 20% compared to the 9.6% increase that the S&P 500 saw. This is even though asset quality is disappointing and shares are trading at high multiples relative to earnings. I won't go so far as to say that it's appropriate to downgrade the company at this time. This is especially true considering some progress that the firm has made when it comes to profit margins. But if we continue to see it outperform the market like this, without a corresponding surge in profitability, a downgrade will become inevitable.

Shares Are Too Pricey For My Liking

The latest data that investors have access to when it comes to Origin Bancorp covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that quarter, deposits for the institution amounted to $8.31 billion. That's actually up a bit compared to the $8.22 billion in deposits that the company had a year earlier. This rise occurred even as high-cost brokered deposits for the company dropped from $80.2 million to a big old goose egg. This means that organic deposit growth was relatively impressive, expanding by 2% year over year.

As deposits increased, other parts of the balance sheet also improved. Take loans as an example. At the end of 2024, the company had $7.48 billion worth of loans on its books. But by 2025, that number had risen to $7.57 billion. Historically speaking, a lot of what the company invests in here would be commercial real estate. It had about $2.52 billion worth of loans under this category in its portfolio at the end of 2025. It also had about $2 billion of residential real estate, followed by $1.99 billion of commercial and industrial loans. Outside of that, its loan portfolio consisted primarily of construction, land, and land development loans; mortgage warehouse lines of credit; and consumer loans.

It would be unwise for us to overlook other parts of its balance sheet. Take securities as an example. In 2025, our candidate had $1.13 billion worth of securities on its books. That compares to the $1.12 billion that it had a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $470.2 million down to $424.2 million. But that was a small price to pay for debt declining from $172.4 million down to just $35.6 million. That decline in debt has actually been part of a larger trend for the company. For context, back in 2022, the firm had $841 million of debt on its books. Management, seeking higher returns in a difficult interest rate environment, figured that reducing overall leverage would improve margins. And honestly, they were right about that.

In the chart above, you can see the net interest margin for the institution. From 2024 to 2025, it skyrocketed from 3.22% to 3.61%. And for the final quarter of 2025, the 3.73% reading that the company had exceeded the 3.65% reported the previous year. This rise was thanks to strength across multiple categories. In the chart below, you can see that while there has been a bit of weakness when it comes to the amount that the company is getting from loans, the returns on taxable securities have been on the rise. Crucially, we have seen changes elsewhere, including a plunge in all but its subordinated debt and a drop in interest-bearing deposits. These declines can be attributed to the fact that, as the Federal Reserve slashed interest rates, many of the liabilities that the company has adjusted lower instantly. Assets, meanwhile, tend to be stickier, especially because some of them are fixed rate and because some of them have interest rate floors.

Normally, you would expect an improvement in net interest margin to translate to a rise in net interest income. But the picture in this case has been a bit more complicated. In 2025 as a whole, for instance, net interest income for the company amounted to $284.7 million. That was actually down from the $292.9 million that the company reported a year earlier. But that decrease, according to the company, was the result of a surge in its provision for credit losses from $7.4 million to $46.3 million. The final quarter of 2025 did see net interest income contract as well. However, this was only because in 2024 the firm booked a $5.4 million benefit for credit losses compared to the $3.2 million revision that it saw in 2025.

Non-interest income has been all over the map. In the final quarter of last year, for instance, it was $16.7 million. That compared to the negative $0.3 million the company saw a year earlier. But in 2024, the firm suffered from a $14.6 million loss on the sale of securities. For the year as a whole, however, the growth was more modest. Regardless, that did push net profits down from $76.5 million in 2024 to $75.2 million in 2025. But the final quarter of the year did a lot of the heavy lifting there, with the $29.5 million generated by the company coming in more than double the $14.3 million that we saw a year earlier.

Even if we adjust for the losses on the sale of investment securities that the company incurred in 2025, we would still get a price-to-earnings multiple for the institution of 14.5. That is meaningfully above the 10 maximum that I typically look for. What's more, three of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart ended up being cheaper. So even on a relative basis, it's not like this is exactly a value play. When it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples, however, the picture is more appealing. The chart below shows the company trading at a slight discount to book value and at just a slight premium on a price-to-tangible-book basis. However, even this puts the company around the middle of the group.

One issue that I have with the company is that its asset quality is not at the level that I would like to see. Take return on assets as an example. I typically like this to be at 1% or greater. But as the first chart below illustrates, Origin Bancorp has a reading of 0.77%. That puts the company below three of the five banks that I compared it to. In the chart below that, you can see that it ranks at the same level when it comes to return on equity. The 6.24% reading that we get is also below the 10 minimum that I typically want in a candidate.

In addition to asset quality coming in below expectations, credit quality is also somewhat disappointing. I typically prefer that no more than 0.50% of both assets and loans are classified as non-performing. But in the most recent quarter, 1.06% of loans and 0.84% of assets met this description. These both represented increases over the 0.99% and 0.81%, respectively, that we saw a year earlier. So in addition to asset quality being worse than I would like to see, Origin Bancorp is also showing a worsening trend in this regard.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available currently, I firmly believe that Origin Bancorp is an interesting company, but not one worthy of a bullish rating right now. In fact, I would make the case that, if anything, a downgrade would be more likely than an upgrade. That is even though management has achieved some really solid success in improving certain parts of the business, and it is because of low asset quality, low credit quality, and a stock price that is high in relation to earnings. Meanwhile, however, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" makes sense.

data.title: Clorox: A Clean Portfolio Holding Through Market Volatility
data.description:
Shares in cleaning products maker Clorox (CLX) have struggled and are trading just off its 52-week lows.

I view the underperformance as a surprise, especially given the broader market volatility and what I would expect as a greater draw to safety in this bear-like trading environment.

Shares in CLX come attached with a safe quarterly dividend that is currently yielding nearly 5%, and I believe there is also attractive upside potential embedded in the stock.

While the company has struggled with uneven demand trends, with its topline hurt by trade-down behavior from core consumers, I believe its necessity-based business model and defensive characteristics will ultimately serve it well in the current uncertain market environment.

CLX Stock Key Metrics

Though shares in CLX are positive YTD, the stock is still down about 30% over the past year. What I find even more shocking, however, is the nearly 50% decline over the past five years.

The company certainly has had its setbacks, including the ongoing trend of consumer trade-down for its core cleaning products. However, I still view the underperformance as overkill, especially for a time-tested and durable customer staple such as CLX.

Despite the underperformance, CLX’s valuation still trades at what many would say is not a deep bargain. Shares currently trade at a 17x forward, for example.

That’s still slightly above the sector median. In addition, CLX’s enterprise value is 12x its forward EBITDA, also a few clicks above the sector. These valuations, however, are significantly discounted to its 5-year averages, and on some of these metrics, I can see CLX rising back up to its mean.

At present, there is broad consensus that CLX is a "Hold" at current levels, with most coverage from the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community in the "Hold" or "Sell" lean.

While analysts on Wall Street are also neutral on the stock, consensus estimates see about 20% upside potential in the stock. In my view, this is attainable, and I can see CLX reaching a target of about $120/share at some point this year.

CLX Operating Performance

CLX’s most recent results were for its Q2 and were for the period ended December 31. In looking at those results, I would say that Q2 looked worse on the surface than they really were underneath.

At the topline, net sales declined 1%, and this was driven largely by lower consumption. However, the management team made it obvious that shipment timing tied to last year’s ERP transition distorted the YOY.

Organic sales were also down 1%, but I don’t really find this overly concerning for a staples name.

The story was similar for margins. Gross margin declined due to higher manufacturing and logistics costs, partially offset by cost savings.

EPS also declined, more significantly for that matter, with diluted EPS down 16% and adjusted EPS down 10%, but this was heavily driven by lapping prior-period cyber insurance recoveries and tax benefits.

From the segment perspective, performance was more mixed. Both Health and Wellness and International reported growth and strong EBIT, while Household and Lifestyle saw softer demand trends tied to lower consumption.

For me, cash flows were one of the most important metrics. More specifically, operating cash flow remained steady YTD. To me, this signals that while reported earnings look pressured, the core cash-generating engine of the business remains intact.

What Is The Outlook For CLX Stock?

CLX did reaffirm its full-year guidance, but what’s important here is understanding why fiscal 2026 looks so soft on paper.

First, roughly 7.5 points of the expected organic sales decline and about $0.90/share of EPS pressure stem from a temporary shipment timing issue tied to last year’s ERP transition. In other words, this is accounting noise, in my view.

CFO Luc Bellet made it clear that this headwind would fully reverse in fiscal 2027, effectively creating an easier comparison and an earnings step-up next year.

In addition, for the back half of this year, CLX is expecting sequential improvement in organic sales, supported by a strong innovation slate, better on-shelf execution now that ERP is behind them, and margin expansion that should be most visible in Q4 as one-time ERP costs roll off and cost savings accelerate.

Then there’s the pending GOJO Industries acquisition, which I can see as one possible long-term catalyst. The addition of the marker of Purell brings an +$800 million, mid-single-digit growth business that is heavily skewed toward B2B channels, and I believe this would perfectly complement CLX’s retail strength.

CLX sees at least +$50 million in cost synergies from the transaction, plus cross-selling and brand investment opportunities that aren’t even in the base case.

All considered, I see CLX moving through 2026 with stronger fundamentals than it entered, and I believe this sets up a much cleaner story into fiscal 2027.

Is CLX Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

In my view, what the market appears to be focusing on right now are the headline declines in sales, margins, and EPS. What I believe is being missed, however, is why those numbers look the way they do.

From what I see, much of the weakness tied to fiscal 2026 is the result of temporary ERP-related shipment timing, lapping prior benefits, and near-term consumer trade-down behavior. I don’t necessarily view these headwinds as structural issues to the business.

I still believe CLX is a portfolio of necessity-based brands that consumers rely on daily, regardless of the economic environment at large. In a market environment rattled by geopolitical tension and volatility, that defensive characteristic becomes even more valuable, in my view.

Moreover, investors are being paid nearly 5% to wait. That dividend is supported by steady cash flow and a management team that has reiterated its commitment to returning capital to shareholders.

Looking ahead, I see improving organic trends in the back half, margin recovery into Q4, and a much cleaner setup into fiscal 2027 as ERP noise reverses. The pending GOJO Industries acquisition could also strengthen the long-term growth profile.

For investors seeking new positioning, I see upside potential of about 20% in CLX, along with an attractive dividend, both with limited downside risk from here. At current levels, I view shares of CLX as a "Buy."

data.title: What The Market Got Wrong With Uber (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Uber Earnings rundown

Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) is back on my radar after the recent pullback. The stock has been in limbo for the past few months, down over 15% year-to-date and touching a recent low of $68.40 on Friday, and is now flat on Monday. Uber went out of the market’s favor after Q4 earnings, which disappointed investors, with revenue of $14.37 billion, above the $14.32 billion consensus, up from $12 billion in the year-ago quarter. The closely watched mobility segment was up 19% year over year to $8.2 billion, slightly missing consensus at $8.3 billion, while delivery revenue was up 30% during the same period to $4.9 billion, above consensus at $4.72 billion.

Delivery was something I flagged when I last covered the stock in September of last year, and it seems to be gaining momentum.

Uber's Q4 net income of $296 million looked weak against the prior year's $6.88 billion, but the comparison doesn’t give the full picture, as the drop was driven by a $1.6 billion pre-tax headwind from equity investment revaluations, higher taxes, and deliberate pricing investments to drive affordability and user acquisition. Strip that out, and the operating picture was clean, with gross bookings of $54.1 billion beating the $53.1 billion consensus; monthly active consumers hit 202 million, up 18% year-over-year, and management expects at least 17% gross bookings growth to $52 billion to $53.5 billion in Q1 '26. The softness in reported profits was the cost of buying growth, and by the bookings and user metrics, it's working.

Trips per vehicle per day on Uber's platform are running 30% higher than standalone first-party AV platforms, with better ETAs. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi also made the structural point that Uber's delivery and freight network allows it to utilize AV vehicles during mobility demand troughs, which is a cross-network utilization advantage that no pure-play AV operator can replicate.

“Uber accelerated into another record-breaking quarter, with more than 200 million monthly users completing more than 40 million trips every day—our largest and most engaged consumer base ever…We enter 2026 with a rapidly growing topline, significant cash flow, and a clear path to becoming the largest facilitator of AV trips in the world,” said Khosrowshahi in the earnings report.

Stock performance and valuation

Along with market uncertainty around the war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the stock is suffering, and mostly moving in sympathy with the broader market. Still, let's look at the glass half full; Uber is the kind of company that gets thrown out with the bathwater in a macro selloff, which is exactly what's happened here. The business hasn't deteriorated, and the market has simply re-rated everything with exposure to consumer discretionary spending and global growth. At current levels, that creates an entry point worth paying attention to.

Uber is down around 7% over the past year, down another 30% over the past 6 months, down 8% over the past month, and down another 8% over the past 5 days alone. Year to date, the story doesn’t look that different for Uber, but it definitely looks worse for Lyft (LYFT) and DoorDash (DASH), as seen below, with both Lyft and Dash down over 30% each, double that of Uber’s pullback.

Valuation has also just gotten way more attractive than it was a month ago, though the business continues to grow. Uber trades at 12.86x forward EV/EBITDA, less than half of DoorDash's 16.98x, and on a business with a 27.39% forward EBITDA growth rate. The most striking single data point is the forward PEG of 0.91, below 1.0, implying the market is not giving Uber full credit for its growth rate, which is unusual for a platform of this scale and quality.

Layer in a levered free cash flow 3-year CAGR of 125.85%, an EBITDA margin of 12.13% against Lyft's negative 0.53%, and a 10.70% EBIT margin that neither competitor comes close to matching, and the profitability profile starts to look like a business that has quietly crossed a maturity threshold the market hasn't fully repriced.

At 2.43x forward EV/Sales and 13.95x price-to-cash flow, investors are paying a reasonable multiple at a point in the cycle where sentiment has temporarily disconnected from fundamentals. The stock is trading at around $69 and has been more or less trading sideways since mid-February, when it also traded at $69. While the stock broke the EMA21 resistance level at $73 last week, it didn’t hold. The stock is also trading below the EMA50 at $76 and the EMA200 at $82. RSI is also nearing oversold territory, though it's not there yet, at 36. With all EMAs trading downwards, the stock remains above that $86.4 low. As long as it holds above that level and gains momentum over the next trading days, I think the risk-reward is favorable here.

Investment thesis

I think the opportunity here outweighs the risks, and the AV story is the real catalyst. Uber now has 20-plus AV partners globally and expects to be operating in 15 cities by year-end, with a deal to invest up to $1.25 billion in Rivian (RIVN) to deploy 50,000 robotaxis already in motion. The strategic logic is airtight since Uber doesn't need to build the cars. Uber works as an operating layer that every AV manufacturer routes through, and it already does.

The company is also now working hand in hand with Nvidia (NVDA), and that is about one of the best partnerships you can have in the AI and autonomy era. Nvidia's DRIVE platform is the compute backbone that most AV developers are building on, and Uber sits at the other end of that stack as the distribution and commercialization layer. According to the press release, back in mid-March, both companies,

“announced an expansion of their autonomous vehicle partnership. The companies, supported by their growing roster of automaker partners, plan to launch a global fleet of entirely NVIDIA software-driven autonomous vehicles, starting in Los Angeles and San Francisco in the first half of 2027 and scaling across 28 cities globally by 2028.”

According to Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang,

“The ‘ChatGPT moment’ for physical AI has arrived—robotic systems can now reason about the complexities of the physical world…Uber is building one of the world’s most expansive autonomous ride-hailing platforms. We are delighted to connect NVIDIA’s large ecosystem of robotaxi-ready partners to the Uber network to bring the magic of robotaxis to cities worldwide.”

The broader AV partnership roster reinforces the network effect dynamic that makes Uber's position defensible. With 20-plus AV partners, spanning Google (GOOG) backed Waymo, Motional, Cruise, Avride, Rivian, and now Nvidia, Uber has avoided the trap of betting on a single technology winner. The platform grows stronger as the ecosystem expands, the opposite of how most hardware-dependent businesses scale.

I’m upgrading the stock to a strong buy at current levels.

Risks

With every investment, there’s risk, and the case with Uber is no different, which is why the bear case deserves a fair hearing, particularly in this macro environment. The most immediate risk for Uber, in my opinion, is consumer spending sensitivity. Uber is a discretionary service at its core, and with U.S. inflation running closer to 3%, and gasoline prices surging on the back of the Hormuz closure, the consumer backdrop is deteriorating in ways that directly pressure trip frequency and average order values.

Still, Uber doesn't own a single car and doesn't pay for a drop of fuel, as the entire cost of vehicle ownership, maintenance, insurance, and gasoline falls on the independent driver. That asset-light structure is precisely what insulates Uber's income statement from the energy shock that is currently devastating traditional transportation businesses. The risk is there, even if it comes indirectly. If gasoline prices rise enough, driver supply could tighten as the economics of driving for Uber deteriorate, pushing up incentive costs to attract and retain drivers.

The stock is now down over 29% since my Buy back in September.

While I wouldn’t be surprised with some near-term volatility with the overall market on edge and no end to the war in sight, I think the risk-reward for Uber is favorable.
